request.question: What year was Sidwell Friends School founded where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years?

title: Josh Hart (basketball)
Friends School of Wilmington
Tandem Friends School
J.C. Luning
Newtown Friends School
Media-Providence Friends School
Alexandria Friends School
Roderick H. Cox
Sidwell Friends School
Virginia Beach Friends School
sentences: Josh Hart (born March 6, 1995) is an American basketball player for the Los Angeles Lakers of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
He played college basketball for Villanova.
As a senior at Sidwell Friends School, Hart posted averages 24.3 points, 13.4 rebounds and 2.8 steals per game and was a highly recruited prospect.
In his sophomore season at Villanova, Hart was named the Big East Tournament most outstanding player.
As a junior, he was named to the All-Big East first team and helped Villanova win the national championship in 2016.
Friends School of Wilmington is an 18 months - 8th grade private/independent Quaker school located in Wilmington, North Carolina.
The school was founded in 1995 by the Wilmington (NC) Friends Meeting and has grown from a middle school for 30 students to a school for 190 students.
Friends School is located on two campuses located 2.9 miles apart with five buildings and eight acres of playgrounds, fields, gardens, woods, and a pond.
Tandem Friends School is a coeducational secondary school founded in 1970 in Albemarle County, Virginia, just outside Charlottesville, by educators John Howard and Duncan Alling.
In 1995, it joined the Friends Council on Education, adopting the educational beliefs and practices of the Quakers.
The current head of school is Whitney Thompson.
The Upper School, grades 9-12, has approximately 100 students, while the Middle School, grades 5-8, has approximately 120 students.
The head of the Upper School is Peter Gaines, and the head of the Middle School is Tom O'Connor.
Tandem is located on 279 Tandem Lane.
The mascot is a Quaker, though the original mascot was a badger.
When the mascot was changed in 2015, there was a good deal of resistance from students concerning the fact that though the school is technically Quaker, very few students are Quaker.
Students felt misrepresented, the more so since the badger was a mascot the students had chosen themselves, whereas the Quaker was imposed upon them.
The logo is the historic main building (originally a plantation house and later a Civil War hospital), which is thought by some to be haunted.
John Christian Luning (December 21, 1863 – September 28, 1928) was a mayor of the town of Leesburg, Florida, during the 1890s and Lake County Commissioner.
He would go on to serve the shortest term as Florida's Commissioner of Agriculture for 14 days before going on to serve as State Treasurer of Florida.

He was part of a group of Florida leaders known as the "Three Musketeers" – along with James B. Whitfield (a state treasurer and state attorney general before serving nearly 40 years on the Florida Supreme Court) and William V. Knott (who held various cabinet positions for nearly 40 continuous years before becoming a political appointee) – and had a bright political future before his sudden death in September 1928.
Newtown Friends School is a coeducational prekindergarten through eighth grade Quaker independent school founded in 1948 and located in Bucks County, Pennsylvania.
Though its name and address indicate it is in Newtown Township, it is physically located in Middletown Township.
Newtown Friends' campus adjoins with that of George School.
Media-Providence Friends School is a Quaker school founded as Media Friends School in Media, Pennsylvania in 1876.
Alexandria Friends School (AFS) was a private, non-profit Friends (Quaker) middle and high school (grades 6-12) located at 3830 Seminary Road in Alexandria, Virginia, United States.
It was founded in 2006 by faculty and staff from Thornton Friends School's Alexandria campus, which closed at the end of the 2005-2006 school year.
The school closed in 2012, despite promises to reopen the following year.
Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.
He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.
Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.
He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.
Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.
Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.
All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.
Virginia Beach Friends School (VBFS) is an independent life-skills and college preparatory day school founded in 1955 under the care of the Virginia Beach Friends Meeting.

Virginia Beach Friends School has more than 200 students enrolled in four divisions – Early School (Cottage, Treehouse, Pre-K and Kindergarten), Lower School (Grades 1-5), Middle School (Grades 6-8), and Upper School (Grades 9-12).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What year was Sidwell Friends School founded where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years?
hop1_docs: Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.  He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.  Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.  He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.

Sidwell Friends School | Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.  Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.  All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.

Roderick Cox (conductor) | Roderick Cox is an African American conductor.  He is currently associate conductor of the Minnesota Orchestra, a position he was appointed to in September 2016 after serving as assistant conductor for a year.

Marvin Chirelstein | Marvin A. Chirelstein (October 8, 1928 - February 16, 2015) was a Professor Emeritus of Law at Columbia Law School, where he taught for nearly 30 years.  He taught a contracts course to first year law students as well as tax and corporate finance courses.  Prior to teaching at Columbia, Professor Chirelstein taught for over 15 years at Yale Law School.  He authored several course books on law.

Joe Cox (American football) | Cox started at Independence High School in Charlotte, North Carolina.  Cox was named to the 2004 "Parade Magazine" All-America Team and "Super Prep" All-Mid Atlantic Team.  He was the North Carolina Gatorade Player of the Year.  Cox was on the "AP" first-team All-State team and was a two-time "Charlotte Observer" Offensive Player of the Year.  He also played in the Shrine Bowl of the Carolinas and impressed scouts by going 31-0 as a starting quarterback.  Cox was a two-time team captain and was the number 7 rated quarterback in the country by "Rivals.com" and Tom Lemming of ESPN.  He set a North Carolina record with 66 touchdown passes while taking his team to its fifth consecutive state championship his senior year.  Cox was named the MVP of the state title game twice.  As a junior, Cox threw for nearly 4,000 yards and over 40 touchdowns.  As a senior, he completed 240 out of 363 passes for over 4,500 yards and only five interceptions.

Roderick Sprague | Roderick Sprague III (February 18, 1933 – August 20, 2012) was a renowned American anthropologist, ethnohistorian and historical archaeologist, and the Emeritus Director of the Laboratory of Anthropology at the University of Idaho in Moscow, where he taught for thirty years.  He had extensive experience in environmental impact research, trade beads, aboriginal burial customs, and the Columbia Basin area.

Priscilla Baird | Priscilla Baird (1828-1904) was a pioneering teacher in Missouri and Illinois and an advocate for girls' education.  She began her career in Shelbyville, Missouri in 1851.  She then taught at Liberty Female College and Lancaster schools before relocating to Illinois during the civil war and teaching at the Springfield High School.  Returning to Missouri, she taught at Ingleside College in Palmyra, Missouri and at Hardin College in Mexico, Missouri.  After completing nearly thirty years of teaching, she founded the Baird College in 1885, where she remained until her retirement in 1897.
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage 1 notes Roderick H. Cox "was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years" and passage 2 states Sidwell Friends School was "founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell." Bridge entity: Sidwell Friends School. Missing fact: the founding year required to answer the question is present in the passages (1883). Retrieval directions: 1) Open the Sidwell Friends School Wikipedia or official history page to confirm the 1883 founding and founder; 2) if needed, open Roderick H. Cox's biography page to corroborate his tenure at Sidwell Friends School.
response.hop2_query:
1) Sidwell Friends School Wikipedia founded 1883 Thomas Sidwell
2) Sidwell Friends School official history founding year
3) Roderick H. Cox Sidwell Friends School biography
hop2_docs: Sidwell Friends School | Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.  Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.  All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.

Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.  He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.  Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.  He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.

Annice Sidwells | Annice Sidwells (1902–2001) was a star of early wireless radio in the UK as a contralto singer.  Sidwells was born in Settle, North Yorkshire, England.  Her talent belied the meagre settings of an amateur operatic society in a remote Yorkshire market town, and Sidwells became a star of the early radio broadcasts of the BBC.

Tom Sidwell | Thomas Edgar Sidwell (30 January 1888 – 8 December 1958) was an English cricketer.  A right-hand batsman and a wicketkeeper, Sidwell made 392 appearances for Leicestershire County Cricket Club between 1913 and 1933.  His 551 catches and 127 stumpings were a county record until beaten by Roger Tolchard, and his keeping skill made him a rival of incumbent national keeper Herbert Strudwick though Sidwell was never selected for England.  Two of his three centuries came in the 1928 season where he hit 1,153 runs, and he batted in both the lower and top order.
Bill Sidwell | Oswald William Thomas Sidwell (born 16 April 1920 in Goulburn, New South Wales) is an Australian former tennis player.

Ken Sidwell | Kenneth B. Sidwell is an American former college head basketball and baseball head coach.  He coached Belmont's basketball team from 1962 to 1964 and again from 1972 to 1974, as well as Tennessee Tech from 1964 to 1969.  In baseball, Sidwell coached Belmont from 1963 to 1964.  He was also the first full-time athletic director in Belmont's history.  He was inducted into Belmont's Hall of Fame as a coach and administrator in 1993.

St Sidwells | St Sidwells is an area east of Exeter city centre in the ward of Newtown.  Formerly a village in its own right, St Sidwells grew in importance along with Exeter thanks to its location on the main cart track between Exeter and the high ground of Stoke Hill and the rich farmland of East Devon.
response.summary_2: Sidwell Friends School was founded in 1883. The school's history names Thomas Sidwell as founder and locates the Quaker school in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years.
